paragraphs.body: I want to highlight how my expectations of city centers, shaped by my experiences in China, clashed with the reality of Pittsburgh’s downtown, and how comparing it with Beijing helped me understand cultural differences in urban design and lifestyle.

Before I visited downtown Pittsburgh, I always thought a city center would have tons of shopping malls. Usually, these malls are located under tall office buildings, and they’d be filled with clothing stores, restaurants, and movie theaters. I pictured myself strolling through the streets, doing some shopping, grabbing dinner, and catching a movie. After all, in China, almost every major city’s downtown has a mix of shopping, dining, and entertainment options all in one spot.

When I was sitting in the car, I felt like downtown Pittsburgh matched what I had imagined—lots of tall buildings, just like in China. But when I walked around, I was surprised to see that most of the buildings were banks and office towers. There weren’t any big commercial complexes where people could shop, relax, or have fun in one place. The restaurants were spread out, and nothing was close together, which was different from what I expected.

Downtown Pittsburgh is quite different from what I imagined. If I compare it to downtown Beijing, Beijing’s city center is surrounded by low-rise buildings because the Forbidden City and Tiananmen Square are at the very center, and there are height restrictions set by the government. But along the streets, you’ll find lots of shops selling food, drinks, and various goods. In contrast, downtown Pittsburgh is mostly office buildings with no major tourist attractions or food stalls. Most people are there to work, not to hang out. Meanwhile, in Beijing, a large part of the crowd downtown every day is made up of tourists.

This cross-cultural comparison really changed how I see city centers. In Beijing, the architecture reflects a deep respect for history and cultural heritage, with most buildings maintaining a classical style. Pittsburgh, on the other hand, has this industrial feel, especially with all the steel bridges connecting the city—symbols of the city’s industrial roots. It made me realize how cities reflect the stories of their pasts. One emphasizes tradition and history, while the other highlights industry and growth.

Comparing these two cities made me more aware of how architecture reflects their unique histories. In Beijing, the classical buildings preserve its rich past, showcasing a deep respect for history through traditional architecture. In Pittsburgh, the steel bridges and industrial structures also tell a story—one of the city’s industrial rises. While the expressions are different—Beijing with its historic landmarks and Pittsburgh with its industrial legacy—both are ways of honoring their cultural heritage. These cities may reflect history through different lenses, but they both carry the weight of the past in meaningful ways.

annotations.comment: This is well-written. You have done a good job in developing a plot for your narrative. I enjoyed reading your description of downtown Pittsburgh. I would suggest elaborating more on your current views of downtown Pittsburgh before comparing it to Beijing's city center. Please also use Chinese words and/or dialogues to enrich the outline. Overall, good job!
